item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so dumb, they're just some especially dangerous crabs. This doesn't need to be an SCP. I wouldn't bat an eye if something like this was discovered today

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shouldn't this be tagged with the "sapient" tag? Either way, simplistic anomaly with a short and sweet article.
+1

item.title: image source
item.content:encoded:
https://www.pmel.noaa.gov/eoi/nemo/explorer/bio_gallery/biogallery-Info.00017.html

This is an example of Macroregonia macrochira, a 'spider crab' that lives in deep-sea hydrothermal vents. It's a female; she's brooding eggs.

I think the picture is PD by virtue of being a work of the US Government, but I wouldn't swear to that.

item.title: Defense
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I kinda like these crabs as it is - we've got too many eldritch abominations and horrible world-eating monstrosities, having some milder stuff does wonders to make the rest stand out.

However, perhaps their rather non-anomalous nature could be acknowledged in the report - maybe a debate over whether or not to reveal these creatures' existence to the public, or at the very least subject them to an analysis of whether or not they should qualify. "We don't see anything overtly supernatural here, but we're gonna look a bit longer until we feel sure." There's already one or two SCPs that do something like that, so why not take advantage of that relative dearth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I echo the many comments saying there's nothing anomalous about this entry. There's a lot of discussion about what does and doesn't work about it. I think I can add a lot to that discussion; I'm a cripple who loves animals and thus spends a great deal of time reading about them. I don't want to necro some old threads, so I'm just gonna go long here. Apologies for length, I wish to be thorough.

+ show block
– hide block

Could this thing occur naturally? LOL, GOD no. But the truth is, that describes a great deal of fictional animals. Including ones that are supposed to be realistic. Very rarely are fictional animals truly accurate when you get down to the science of "could this be real?" This animal could not be real (at least here, now, naturally), no. However, I also don't see anything anomalous about it. Maybe I don't understand the meaning, but I took it to mean that every SCP must be paranormal or supernatural in some way. It must violate some known laws of science. This does not. Honestly? The most unrealistic part of this creature is something I don't see anyone touching on: its bit about only eating live prey. The notion that they stop caring once they can't torture it anymore is either supposed to be the anomalous part, or just ridiculous. No predator is going to go hungry rather than eat something it JUST killed. Nature is a brutal horror show of wanton suffering and hardship. You don't get to be picky. This behavior would not last. The specimens which did not have this nonsensical hangup would be more successful, and the trait would be quickly bred out. Or the species would die clinging to it.

About an arthropod this large moving outside water? You're assuming that, internally, this creature functions just as a normal crustacean does. Until we've dissected them and learned their internal anatomy, we can't assume they share the same traits as real crustaceans. The reason arthropods have this issue with moving is because an exoskeleton has only the shittiest option for joints: pin joints, which must focus all the pressure on one small area. Every known animal has either an exoskeleton (i.e. a skeleton outside the body) or an endoskeleton (i.e. a skeleton inside the body). If it has even a rudimentary endoskeleton, then sky's the limit on size. If the shell is particularly thick then its sheer mass might slow down movement, but hey, tortoises are a pretty successful life form.

Oh, that "parrots human speech" thing. Bad. That whole thing about parrots just mimicking and not understanding? Total bullshit. Foundation's scientists wouldn't be using such phrases. At least not the zoological ones.

Brains. that's another part of them that, while it isn't anomalous in and of itself, doesn't make sense. Crustaceans' brains are so simple that they can't even feel pain. A crustacean as smart as SCP-098 would be like a dog as smart as Stephen Hawking. It's like most of the rest of its biology: it makes sense, but not on a crab. If I were a Foundation scientist working on this, I wouldn't be focusing on the crab itself. My question would be, where did it COME from? It's clearly alien. Whether extraterrestrial or extradimensional or what have you. It seems like a real animal that could have evolved (even its silly torture fetish if its prey was ridiculously abundant), but it did not do so here! The question then becomes, does the Foundation want to participate in an alien coverup even when the alien objects in question are not paranormal? Whatever your answer to that question, is the answer to "should this be an SCP?" Either way, as I said, Foundation's scientists would be looking into its origin as best they can. UFO sightings in the area, anything that might be a portal or a bend in space-time, has there been any odd disappearances in the area (not the victims of the crabs, I mean someone who might've went to wherever these came from), that sort of thing. These crabs aren't the fire, they're the smoke.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: Re: what....
item.content:encoded: So it all boils down to several crabs(?) endowed with an unnaturally large size, intelligence comparable to a pack of wolves and knife-like appendages. I can accept this, it poses an ample threat to the normality within the constructs of current human sciences. Good job. +1

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show
- Hide

To link to a tale featuring a mentioned character, I propose the following edit:

Dr. Mann has taught several of them simple tricks, and they seem to understand the meanings of several commands.

to

Dr. Mann has taught several of them simple tricks, and they seem to understand the meanings of several commands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe what this entry needs is something that makes the crabs a bit more unique. As it stands, they seem like a slightly anomalous version of a naturally generated species. Also, without a uniqueness factor, their containment defies belief: Either the Foundation failed to contain every single specimen or this species is entirely limited to the few numbers secured by the Foundation (given the dimensions of the containment unit). Neither option seems reasonable in-world. A few solutions from the top of my head (meaning they are bad, just meant to be examples):

  • They're mutated subspecies of a known animal, endemic to the place they were found. Cause of mutation unknown. Maybe make the beach where they were found the SPC/containment zone and the individual crabs SPC098-1…s.
  • They are a subset of visibly altered or constructed lifeforms (by unknown means).
  • They are not crabs, but crab-like creatures alien to our reality.

In short, an explanation or elaboration that makes believable the limited number of specimens of the creature, while introducing an enigma that makes them worthy of being an SCP instead of just an anomalous creature.

item.title: Re: what....
item.content:encoded:
one meter is leg-to-leg, not across the body

Oh, I assumed the measurement was leg-to-leg here as well, yeah in that case the surgeon crabs are probably just barely too big.

item.title: Re: what....
item.content:encoded:
Hmm….

I have a feeling that SCP-098 instances are a deviation from Japanese Spider Crabs. Both of them are 40 cm across, and can grow to 60 tall. However, SCP-098 has the added benefit of extremely complicated hunting patterns.

So yea, SCP-098 is anomalous.

item.title: Re: what....
item.content:encoded:
Coconut crabs are just about as large across as 098 (one meter is leg-to-leg, not across the body). However, they're not nearly as tall as the surgeon crab. Similarly, arthropleura was very long and wide, but extremely flat, allowing it to spread that mass out.

An arthropod as massive as this would not be able to move out of the water. It certainly would not be fast enough to be an active predator.

item.title: Re: what....
item.content:encoded:
Its size is literally impossible and breaks the laws of physics.

Coconut crabs can be larger, up to a meter across, and they aren't even quite at the limits for exoskeletons from the square-cube law although they are near the limit for branchiostegal lungs in the current atmosphere.

When conditions where better for arthropods Arthropleura was a millipede 2.5 m long and 45 cm wide.

So the given size here isn't anomalous and certainly isn't impossible.

That, and the fact that there's no plausible way for something like this to have evolved, makes it an SCP.

Cephalopods are invertebrates that are this smart. Dromeosaurs evolved knife-like claws a hundred million years ago.

It would represent a totally unexpected lineage, but so did the archea.

item.title: Re: what....
item.content:encoded: Its size is literally impossible and breaks the laws of physics. That, and the fact that there's no plausible way for something like this to have evolved, makes it an SCP.

item.title: Re: what....
item.content:encoded:
I think the problem is "Why doesn't the Foundation just leak this through normal scientific channels?" There's nothing here, seemingly, that breaks science, other than these being really smart for a crustacean (which isn't impossible; just really unlikely) and some very technical things about sound and lungs that this article doesn't directly address anyway.

Which is often a problem with less supernatural articles. I can see why this one doesn't quite get there for some.

item.title: Re: what....
item.content:encoded:
How is this anomalous in any way at all? They just seem like an ocean's version of a pack of wolves, just smaller. This seems like something that would happen back in the Cretaceous period.

But this isn't the Cretaceous period, isn't it? And wolves like, don't have this expert precision that crabs don't normally have.

I see absolutely no way how the Foundation would be worried about a few crabs that are stranger than the average crab you find under the sand..

They are strange enough to break normality for a civilian, and thus are contained.

if the Foundation will bother to deal with these they should just start classifying grizzly bears as SCPs

Grizzly bears aren't anomalous, they act like normal bears. The definition of anomalous is as follows:

something that deviates from what is standard, normal, or expected.

These deviate from normal and crab behavior. Like seriously, who would expect a crab to eat stuff like that?

item.title: what....
item.content:encoded:
How is this anomalous in any way at all? They just seem like an ocean's version of a pack of wolves, just smaller. This seems like something that would happen back in the Cretaceous period.

I see absolutely no way how the Foundation would be worried about a few crabs that are stranger than the average crab you find under the sand.. if the Foundation will bother to deal with these they should just start classifying grizzly bears as SCPs

I'll admit, I was brought back here by the Bellerverse canon. Still doesn't change how cool this skip is. Honored to get this to +200, though I'm surprised the entry hadn't exceeded the value already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aww, Mann taught them tricks?

The little things seem more adorable than scary!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is a really cool SCP. I admit I am a bit biased however, because I happen to have the hobby of making up creatures with appropriate biology for their environment. Although they don't really endanger normality. Yes, I admit, smart parrot crabs are weird, but so is any other creature when its first discovered. But still, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I recently re-watched crab battle so now anything crab related brings unintentional giggles. Still, this is well done. It's almost believable, yet totally anomalous. +1 for the mighty crab!

item.title: Re: Pretty please.
item.content:encoded: Quite possibly, the rules may have been a bit more lax 5-ish years ago - when that post was made.

item.title: Re: Pretty please.
item.content:encoded: I thoguht that images had to be spoilered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for pointing out the square cube law! I'm a tabletop RPer, and it often drives me nuts how few people realize that weight increases counterintuitively fast with size. That being said, I'm fine with oversized arthropods, but the needed adaptations would have to be a lot more profound than size! Giant Spiders and crabs might or might not seem boring as SCPs, but they are NEVER mundane.
And if you were wondering, no. I can't remember the predicted size limit for normal exoskeletal support in terrestrial animals…

item.title: Re: Question about relation to Clef's 001 proposal...
item.content:encoded: Someone did that in one of the Canon story series. Competitive Eschatology. It's only a small part, but check it out if you have a chance.

item.title: Re: Question about relation to Clef's 001 proposal...
item.content:encoded: No, I am with you, I thought an apocolypse of crabs would have been great XD

item.title: But imagine....
item.content:encoded: The first time I read this I read in their descriptions that they were 40 by 60 METERS, not centimeters. I literally jumped out of my desk….Good god that's a scary thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This comment saved article from my-1, but 098 is so meh that I cannot give anything more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The anomalous properties of these things is that 1) they could not have evolved naturally given our understanding of crustacean evolutionary history, since they demonstrate characteristics normally found in completely different phylum that would require absurd levels of horizontal gene transfer for them to develop within the last several dozen million years, and 2) some of their characteristics are physically impossible, such as their size violating the square cube law. They physically should not be able to move.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it just for Clefs proposal requiring these to somehow "activate" to end the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm I'm not sure if this is necro posting, but I agree with most of the detractors on this page. Despite being well written, nothing seems really abnormal in the article. Using silica to make 'glass' claws is a bit unusual but diatoms and nettles use silicates in their biology, several anthropods smaller than these hunt in packs. Also, don't many species of predators employ lures or mimicry to get food? Some lightbugs flash their bulbs in a different species' display to attract foolish males to eat them, and spiders have lungs (Primitive lungs but lungs nonetheless) Maybe describing that testing indicates that their intelligence exceds capabilities of their brain's mass and compare it to a higher order animal's? Describe inaccuricies in it's DNA? A few lines could keep it the same BUT give them the dressing up they need?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

Well done, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my God this gave me the major creeps.

As for the several people before me pointing out that there is nothing anomalous about this, may I direct you to SCP-408? It's an old SCP, sadly not very well known, but one of my favourites nevertheless simply for its beautiful characterization. If you think about it, its situation is exactly the same as this one. It's an animal that has an ability it should not have, PLUS hive mind intelligence. Butterflies aren't supposed to be that smart. Crabs are not supposed to be this terrifying.

And yet, I don't think anyone would bitch at 408 for not being an SCP, because it so clearly is. I think it's the intelligence factor that makes the Foundation consider it a threat. I mean, if you take huge, hybrid super-lizards spawned in governmental test tubes that are so hardy they can survive the apocalypse to roam the wastes — the Deathclaws in Fallout — they'd still probably not become SCPs, because they're just animals. But then, if there's a chance of another intelligent race arising on the planet— well… Intelligence is the only thing we're good at, as animals, so if a competing series develops it and manages to be small enough (like crab or butterfly hiveminds) to hide somewhere we can't find them for a while, we'd probably be quite proper fucked.

item.title: Re: Question about relation to Clef's 001 proposal...
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for clearing that up for me, both of you, though it kinda dashes my hopes of them somehow creating a hailstorm of demonically posessed fungus monsters and crabs… Which I think would be hilarious and terrifying at the same time.
…
Sorry, I have a strange mind xD

item.title: Re: Question about relation to Clef's 001 proposal...
item.content:encoded:
Astonishingly enough, it was a Gateway to Hell:

http://web.archive.org/web/20080810063008/http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/scp-098

A really bad Gateway to Hell, where I can easily see why they got rid of it.

item.title: Re: Question about relation to Clef's 001 proposal...
item.content:encoded: The old 995 was a box that had a fuckton of evil in it. I have no clue what the elder 098 was.

item.title: Question about relation to Clef's 001 proposal...
item.content:encoded: Was there another object here at the point that the article about the Garden of Eden & the Cherub was written? I kinda miss the connection between this and 995 and that particular 001 here otherwise… I mean, 616, I get, since it seems to be some sort of gateway to Hell, but where do the surgeon crabs and some strange group of mold monsters fit together with that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They can talk. They're half again as big as the largest possible land arthropod. They employ a hunting strategy that's unlike any other creature on Earth. They're much smarter than a crustacean has any right to be (and if you'll notice, when they were first captured, the Foundation suspected they were even smarter). None of this makes sense if they evolved naturally. The only reason this isn't pointed out in the article is that it shouldn't need to be. It should be pretty obvious these things can't be naturally-occurring creatures.

I mean, yes, obviously some people are having trouble getting it. But since it's at +62, I have to imagine most readers understand these points without needing to be told.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this really is not interesting or abnormal

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 to that suggestion. Would really help to bring the article together. Maybe they were someone's science project that was supposed to be trained as actual surgeons but the crabs ran amuck and escaped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps something like that could be noted then. It could even make an interesting addendum from a research assistant on how these creatures seem to be engineered. Right now, the article just seems somewhat lacking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thing is, nothing on Earth even approaches this. They are crustaceans with larynxes and lungs. They communicate vocally and are intelligent (if not as much as humans). Their preferred method of feeding is to make a coordinated attack and slowly kill the victim by cutting them up and sealing up the wounds afterwards. None of these things on its own is inherently anomalous, but the fact that everything about it is so different from any commonly known animal with no evolutionary history to speak of would definitely make these worth containing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't see anything truly anomalous, apart from these being oddly-intelligent. There's actual animals that can mimic sounds, most can learn basic tricks, and many are clearly capable of memory. It's really not something the Foundation would bother with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted because it's well written and I like crabs.
However, I agree with the majority opinion that these buggers simply aren't that abnormal.
Other eusocial organisms exist in real life that are much more dangerous than these, and stranger to boot.
It's interesting, I'll admit, but why would the Foundation bother? "Better to lock up all the venomous snakes, it'd protect humanity better." Obviously not, but you see my meaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Given a name like the Surgeon Crabs, I'd foreseen something a little more… surgically oriented. Perhaps the crabs could have an innate knowledge of the biology of a vast number of species, surpassing even our own? Maybe they could be even somewhat cooperative if treated correctly? Just some suggestions.

As is, I have a softshell spot for crabs, so interesting crabs get my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with this article needing something extra. There doesn't seem to be a real reason the Foundation would have this. It seems more a job for animal control. I think it would be better if there was something the Foundation was interested in about the viscous mucus that these crabs spit out. Also more description about their knife hands would be interesting. Even just a transcript of what the crabs say to foundation members during feeding could liven this up a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
nothing outside the realm of normal biology

Pretty much sums it up. I can't really see them pose any threat to normalcy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside form being smarter than the average crab there really isn't all that much anomalous to these (I suppose implementing bladed tools rather than claws could be seen as anomalous but it's nothing outside the realm of normal biology.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These sure are some nasty crabs, but I don't really understand why they are the Foundation's concern. Am I missing something here?

item.title: Oh you
item.content:encoded:
haha, what I mean is that they should really have something to make more like a threat, the only real shocking part is they keep their preys alive for hours while devoring them and the fact they can adapt easily to their environment and learn patterns, but they are nothing that average population couldn't handle, just like "shut doors and windows at night, if you see one call for help and be sure to kill them all" I really have seen more dangerous things around where I live, I bet this SCP could be even smashed with a rock, that's why the idea of giving them a high breed rate could be a good adding and they would be more like "if we leave them alone things could get pretty nasty since they can bred very fast and also adapt easily to be more efficient hunters"

It's not about making them like little unstoppable bloodthirsty demolishers, it's about adding them something else, even a little change can make a big difference

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Better yet, let's make them radioactive! That's really dangerous, right? It's bound to make them more scary!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you should add some other threatening to this SCP features, like incredibly high breed rate , high speed movement in short distances or even the ability of mimic the envivorement ( like a chameleon), or anything else to making them more dangerous than an animal kingdom curiosity.

Dr. Mann has taught several of them simple tricks, and they seem to understand the meanings of several commands. Testing will continue.

You should consider adding a file concerning this, just to make the info about the SCP more complete about it's capabilities.

sorry for my bad english

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: athropods

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've edited this old article for tone and content.

item.title: SCP?
item.content:encoded: This seems like a creature that could very likely be on earth. Nothing really ABNORMAL about it, in my opinion. There are plenty of creatures on earth more dangerous than this one and the Foundation doesn't take those in.

item.title: Well played
item.content:encoded:
can take hours or days
rash of child disappearances

Fridge Horror kicked in and I thought I'd share. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My god… They're like tiny predatory hatbots.

item.title: Re: Pretty please.
item.content:encoded:
…Um, what?

Anyway, I would suggest that the line "They also have a remarkable range of vocalizations." be moved down a couple paragraphs to the section discussing their vocal communication. It feels quite out of place tacked onto the end of a description of their habitat.

item.title: Pretty please.
item.content:encoded:
Please refer to instruct, divulge and apply the following steps:

+ show block
– hide block

Is not Keter but nobody knows if a item will be reclassified.

e: Put image in collapsible. —Photosynthetic

item.title: Re: why?
item.content:encoded: Nope, haven't even read that one yet.

item.title: Re: why?
item.content:encoded: Are you talking about that flying deer?
